common_name: Violetta di Firenze Eggplant (Florentine Purple-White Striped Italian Heirloom)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil warms to at least 70°F and nights are reliably above 55°F; Violetta di Firenze is an Italian heirloom eggplant from the Florence area of Tuscany, producing large, round to slightly oval, deeply ribbed fruits with dramatic lavender-purple and white vertical striping — one of the most visually stunning and historically important eggplant varieties in Italian kitchen garden tradition.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost at 80°F; transplant into warm, fertile, well-drained soil with consistent moisture; Violetta di Firenze produces fewer, larger fruits than Asian types — typically 2–4 fruits per plant at a time — harvest when fruits reach 4–6 inches across and the skin is still glossy; do not allow to over-ripen (seeds enlarge and flesh becomes bitter). Violetta di Firenze (Florentine Violet) is a traditional Italian heirloom eggplant from the Florence area of Tuscany, where it has been grown for generations as the quintessential garden eggplant of the region — so important locally that it appears in Florentine recipe traditions and Renaissance-era botanical descriptions of the kitchen garden. The variety is immediately recognized by its dramatic appearance: very large (6–10 inches across), round to pear-shaped, deeply ribbed fruits with beautiful vertical striping in shades of lavender-violet and white; no other commonly grown eggplant has the same combination of large round shape, deep ribbing, and purple-white striping. The flesh is creamy-white with a mild, sweet, slightly nutty flavor with less bitterness than many eggplant varieties — the large cavity and abundant flesh make it particularly suited for stuffing (ripieno), slicing and grilling, or layering in the classic Italian Parmigiana di Melanzane. The plant is robust and tall (30–48 inches) and requires staking; it is adapted to long, warm Mediterranean summers and performs well in Gulf Coast zone 8b–10 conditions with the hot summers they provide.

common_name: Orient Express Eggplant (AAS Early Japanese-Type Hybrid)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Orient Express is an All-America Selections award-winning Japanese-type eggplant producing long (8–10 inch), slender, dark purple fruits in 58–68 days from transplant — one of the earliest-maturing eggplants available — making it the top choice for northern gardeners with short seasons and for Gulf Coast gardeners who want eggplant harvest to begin before peak summer heat reduces fruit set.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost at 80°F; transplant into warm, fertile, well-drained soil; Orient Express benefits from staking in windy locations; harvest fruits when 6–10 inches long and the skin is glossy dark purple — do not allow to over-ripen; continuous picking encourages continued fruit production. Orient Express is an All-America Selections (AAS) award-winning F1 hybrid Japanese-type eggplant that combines the long, slender fruit shape, thin skin, and tender flesh of Asian eggplant varieties with very early maturity (58–68 days from transplant) — a combination that makes it highly productive across a wide range of climates. The long (8–10 inch), slender (1.5–2 inch diameter) fruits have glossy, near-black purple skin and white, very tender flesh with minimal seeds and virtually no bitterness — the thin skin makes Orient Express excellent for eating with skin on in stir-fries, Japanese-style preparations (nasu dengaku — miso-glazed grilled eggplant), grilling, and dishes where the skin texture would be noticeable in a thick-skinned globe eggplant. The early maturity is particularly valuable for Gulf Coast and Southern gardeners who want to get the eggplant harvest in before peak summer heat reduces fruit set, and for northern gardeners who need production in 60–70 days rather than 80–90 days. Highly productive — a well-grown plant can produce 6–10 fruits at once — and the early season timing allows for multiple successive harvests.

common_name: Tronchuda Cabbage (Portuguese Cabbage, Sea Kale Cabbage)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost for spring planting, or start in midsummer for fall harvest 60–75 days before first fall frost; Tronchuda is a traditional Portuguese cabbage/kale that represents an intermediate type between heading cabbage and kale — it produces a loose, open rosette of large, heavily veined, gray-green leaves with prominent white midribs and a mild, sweet, buttery flavor that is considered among the finest brassica vegetables in Iberian cuisine.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant 18–24 inches apart in rich, well-drained, consistently moist soil; harvest outer leaves progressively to extend the harvest season; the large white midribs can be cooked separately and have a flavor somewhat resembling chard. Tronchuda (Brassica oleracea var. costata) is a traditional Portuguese vegetable with centuries of cultivation in Portugal, particularly in the Azores and coastal regions of Portugal where it is a staple of Caldo Verde (the famous Portuguese green soup) alongside the more familiar Galician version using curly kale. Tronchuda is classified as a distinct variety of Brassica oleracea that sits between cabbage, kale, and kohlrabi — it produces a large, loose rosette of heavily crinkled, gray-green leaves (not forming a tight head like cabbage) with exceptionally prominent white midribs that can be quite thick and juicy. The flavor is notably sweet and mild compared to other brassica vegetables, with the characteristic savoy/collard family character but more delicate and buttery — considered superior to standard kale for soups and slow-cooked brassica dishes by Portuguese cooks. Tronchuda is cold-hardy, producing some of its finest quality after light frosts, and it can be harvested over an extended season by removing outer leaves while the center continues to produce. Also called 'sea kale cabbage' in some English references (not to be confused with true sea kale, Crambe maritima).

common_name: Dwarf Blue Scotch Kale (Blue-Green Curly Dwarf Kale)
scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. sabellica 'Dwarf Blue Scotch'

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer 8–12 weeks before first fall frost, or in early spring 4–6 weeks before last frost; Dwarf Blue Scotch is one of the most cold-hardy and compact kale varieties available — the low-growing (12–18 inch), intensely blue-green, finely curled plants survive temperatures well below 0°F in established gardens and produce some of their finest quality after repeated frost exposure through winter.

directions: Sow directly 1/2 inch deep in cool weather and thin to 12–18 inches apart; Dwarf Blue Scotch is very compact and can be planted more closely than full-size curly kales; harvest outer leaves continuously as cut-and-come-again; the plants tolerate temperatures well below 0°F and produce through winter in zones 5–7 without protection. Dwarf Blue Scotch is an old heirloom curly kale variety that combines three notable characteristics: exceptional cold hardiness, compact plant habit, and an attractive, intensely blue-gray-green leaf color that gives it its name. The plants grow only 12–18 inches tall with tightly packed, finely curled and ruffled leaves of a distinctive blue-green color — sometimes described as 'glaucous' or 'sea blue' — that deepens and intensifies after cold exposure, making Dwarf Blue Scotch one of the most visually attractive kales in the winter garden. The flavor is traditional curly kale — somewhat peppery and earthy raw, becoming sweet and mellow after frost exposure, with the classic kale character that mellows beautifully with cooking in soups, stews, sautés, and traditional Scottish 'kail' preparations. The 'Scotch' name reflects the deep Scottish culinary tradition of kale (kail in Scots dialect), where kale was so central to the diet that the kitchen garden was historically called the 'kailyard' and kale broth (kail brose) was a daily staple. The dwarf habit makes it ideal for raised beds, intensive planting, and smaller gardens where space is limited.

common_name: Osaka Purple Mustard (Japanese Purple Leaf Mustard)

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer or fall (zones 7–11) or early spring (zones 3–7); Osaka Purple is a distinctive Japanese mustard green with round, semi-blistered, bright purple-red leaves — the color intensifies in cool weather, making it one of the most visually striking cool-season greens; the flavor is milder than large-leaved Southern mustard types when young, making it excellent for fresh salads and Asian-style applications.

directions: Sow directly 1/4 inch deep in cool weather; thin to 6–12 inches for mature plants or sow thickly for baby leaf harvest at 3–4 inches; the rich purple color intensifies dramatically as temperatures cool (40–55°F); harvest outer leaves for cut-and-come-again production or harvest whole plants at the baby leaf stage. Osaka Purple is a Japanese mustard green variety with notably different characteristics from the large-leaved American mustard green types — rather than elongated, flat leaves, Osaka Purple produces compact, round, slightly blistered leaves (mildly puckered like a miniature Savoy cabbage) with an intense purple-red color on the upper surface that deepens in cold weather, making it among the most visually attractive winter vegetables for Gulf Coast and Southern gardens. The 'Osaka' name references Japan's second-largest city and its long vegetable-growing tradition. The flavor is milder and more delicate than large-leaved mustard greens — a gentle mustard note and pleasant sweetness that make it excellent raw in salads and fresh spring rolls, as a substitute for radicchio in salad mixes, or lightly stir-fried in Japanese and Korean cooking. Osaka Purple is one of the most visually useful plants for fall and winter salad mixes, providing intense purple-red color that contrasts beautifully with pale green lettuces and white mizuna; it pairs particularly well with arugula, watercress, and baby spinach for color and flavor contrast in mesclun-style mixes.
